"Feed him, please; he's seconds away from fainting," she urged, and all of his teammates glanced at him with concern. They did their best to make Ishan sit and coax him into eating. They might have succeeded, but he had no recollection of it—only of drifting off into a haze of sleep.

After meeting the doctor, everything became a blur. He vaguely remembered waking up on a couch and, upon opening his eyes, saw Shubman lying unconscious a few feet away on a hospital bed. Ishan had no idea how he ended up in Shubman's hospital room. A few minutes later, several doctors entered while he continued to gaze at Shubman in a daze.

Noticing the doctors approaching his bed, he observed their every move and expression. "Here, drink this," one of them said, breaking his intense staring. He turned his head to the right and saw the same doctor from earlier sitting beside him with a glass of strange-smelling liquid.

"What?" was all Ishan managed to say. "Drink this. You'll feel better. You were too exhausted yesterday, both physically and mentally. You need to recover," she explained, smiling as if talking to a child. Ishan appreciated being treated that way at that moment. He took the glass and drank it in one gulp. Surprisingly, it didn't taste as foul as it smelled.

Feeling refreshed, he wanted to ask what it was, but he was also tempted to close his eyes and just sit in peace. When he opened his eyes again, the other doctors had left the room, and the female doctor was checking on Shubman.

"Is he better?" Ishan asked. The doctor smiled. "Yes, he will regain consciousness today, if my estimation is correct. And, not to boast, but I have never been wrong with my estimations before." For the first time since the previous day, Ishan genuinely smiled. Now fully aware of his surroundings, he could process everything, and that felt good.

"You know, when he was brought in yesterday, he was unconscious but actively screaming at the top of his lungs," the doctor continued. "Hmm?" Ishan replied, intrigued. "Well, he seemed to be regaining his memories. He was saying a lot of things that didn't make any sense—just random words and phrases he might have heard or said throughout his life."

"This kind of response is not uncommon in such conditions. We doctors are always prepared for situations like this. Along with the random phrases, he was also mentioning names of people he may have known. However..." She glanced at Ishan, smiling. "But?" he asked, now fully invested in the conversation.

"There was one name he mentioned so frequently that it stuck in our minds. Don't get me wrong; it's common for someone in his state to repeat a single name, but he wasn't just saying it—he seemed to be having a conversation that we couldn't understand because it wasn't in a human language. Still, we caught the name."

"And trust me when I say that if you ask everyone who was present at that moment, they will all mention the same name, even though he had said dozens of others. It became quite amusing at one point." She laughed wholeheartedly. Ishan looked at her in stunned silence. "So, my question is, do you know who this person is?"

"I... what? You didn't tell me the name," Ishan responded, confused. "Oh. Right, I didn't." She paused, staring at him, clearly expecting an answer. "You want me to tell you the name?" she said, sounding a bit let down as if it should have been obvious.

"Yeah," Ishan replied, feeling paranoid about what she would say. "It was 'Ishan.' Do you know someone by that name?" Her smile was no longer sarcastic; it felt sweet, mixed with something Ishan couldn't quite identify. Before he could process her words, she quietly left the room.

I sincerely apologize, everyone. I got caught up in some personal matters and haven't been able to provide any updates. I regret my untimely and impolite absence. I will do my best to upload a few more chapters this week, as I may get busy again next week. Thank you for your patience, and I promise to work hard to ensure you don't have to wait as long in the future.

Title: Is he okay?
Chapter_Text:
I have no idea about what I have written in this chapter. Please excuse this brain rot for now. I promise to serve the next chapter better. For now, I ask for your understanding, and I appreciate your continued support. Additionally, please consider voting for the story. Thank you!

Ishan had been alone for no more than a minute when he heard a commotion at the door. He saw a few of his teammates approaching him. "Hey, are you okay? We left yesterday because the doctors asked us to. How are you--" "Why are you blushing?" Hardik interrupted Siraj mid-sentence, which made everyone look at Ishan curiously.

"What?" Ishan sounded so confused that it only made everyone more puzzled. "You're blushing, Ishan. Are you sick? Do you have a fever?" Abhishek asked with concern as he moved closer to check his forehead. "I'm fine," Ishan replied, trying to dodge the palm that was extending toward him.

"It doesn't look like the sick kind of blush, does it?" Hardik had a tendency to know Ishan too well, and that made Ishan uncomfortable. He glared at Hardik, but unfortunately, this only heightened everyone's suspicion.

Just as they were about to delve deeper into the discussion, Shubman's parents entered the room with Shahneel. "What are we doing at the gate?" Shahneel seemed more relaxed today and was even in the mood for joking, which made Ishan feel happy. It appeared that everyone was feeling better than they had the day before, and for good reason.

"Well, we were trying to figure out why Ishan is blushing," Replied Rishabh with a look that screamed 'slap me if you can' on his face. "Oh." Shahneel turned to Ishan. "I'm not blushing, okay? Maybe I just slept too much," he said quickly, but it was clear that none of them believed him.

However, they felt they couldn't contest it in front of Shubman's mother, who clearly favored Ishan. Ultimately, Ishan emerged victorious, and they dropped the topic after Shubman's mother shot a glare at Shahneel when she was about to argue with Ishan's unconvincing explanation.

"Are you feeling well?" she asked Ishan as she entered the room. "Yeah," he replied with a smile and a hug. They all gathered around the unconscious Shubman, sitting quietly together. The room was enveloped in an uncomfortable silence.

After a couple of hours, half the team decided it was time to leave the hospital, urging Ishan to come with them so he could freshen up at Hardik's place before returning in the evening. Although Ishan hesitated at first, he eventually relented after a lot of persuasion. He agreed to go with Hardik while a few other members would stay behind with Shubman's family.

Once Ishan left, everyone became involved in different conversations, and the previous awkward silence faded away.

Just when they thought everything was going smoothly and they were safe from any more surprises, destiny had other plans. They were sitting around when they heard muffled voices coming from Shubman's bed. Quickly, everyone turned their heads toward him; he was still unconscious but seemed to be saying something that was barely audible.

Siraj pressed the emergency button as the family gathered around Shubman, straining to decipher his words. The nurses soon ushered them out of the room while the doctors began checking Shubman's vitals. Shubman's mother looked like she might faint at any moment, while his sister appeared to be the rock that everyone wished they had in such a situation.

"Should we call Ishan?" Rishabh asked, his expression resembling someone who had just been forced into swallowing bitter gourd juice. "No," Abhishek replied instantly, and everyone else nodded in agreement. "He just went home; I doubt he even had time to freshen up properly. We should keep him out of this for now," Shahneel added, and it made the most sense.

There was nothing more to discuss. No counterarguments. Ishan was to be kept out of this, and that was final. They didn't want him to feel tense again, especially after it had taken them hours to convince him to leave in the first place. The chatter faded as quickly as it began. Everyone was too busy anxiously staring at the door to Shubman's hospital room.

After about 15 minutes, a lady doctor emerged. Their hearts raced as they watched her approach. "Don't look so stressed; it's a good sign. He will regain consciousness in a few minutes. He'll be okay," she reassured Shahneel with a pat on the arm, smiling at the rest of them before leaving them to process the information.

They exchanged hopeful glances. Perhaps this was, indeed, a good sign. Just as they were caught between celebrating and waiting for Shubman to wake up, the rest of the doctors and nurses left the room as well. They didn't seem stressed at all, which felt like a very positive indication.

"He is fine. He just woke up," the doctor in charge informed them as they approached him. Nothing could compare to the joy they felt in that moment. Their faces shone brightly, almost blinding anyone who happened to walk by.

"Can we meet him then?" Shahneel asked, hope evident in her voice. "Yes, you can, but please make sure that too many people don't crowd him at once, and avoid asking him any questions. He can't strain his brain too much right now. You'll be able to talk to him, but just keep it relaxed," the doctor responded.

"Okay, is there anything else we need to keep in mind?" Rishabh asked very seriously, though coming from him, it didn't sound serious at all. "Nothing else, just that. Also, you can speak to him normally again after we check on him in three hours. If everything goes well, there will be no more restrictions," the doctor instructed as he turned to leave. Just as they were about to proceed towards Shubman's room, the doctor paused and turned back to them.

"Um, who is Ishan among you?" That question caught them by surprise for a moment before Abhishek replied, "He went home for a bit. Why?" "Oh, that's good. Make sure he doesn't return for at least three hours." "Why?" This was puzzling. Why would the doctor want to keep Ishan from seeing Shubman? How did he even know Ishan's name?

"Don't worry, it's nothing serious. It just seems like Shubman is most eager to meet this Ishan guy, and we believe that Ishan could trigger some memories in Shubman that we'd rather he not realize yet. Could you also avoid mentioning his name in front of Shubman?" They all exchanged confused glances, unsure of what the doctor meant.

"Okay, you don't seem to understand. Wait here; I'll send someone to explain things more clearly. This isn't exactly my strong suit," the doctor said before leaving, leaving them all pondering what they had just heard. After a minute, the female doctor from earlier approached them with a smile.

"Hi," she said, stifling a laugh at their bewildered expressions. "Let me break it down for you. Sometimes, when someone just starts to regain their memories, certain parts of those memories are stronger than others. Doctors typically recommend that patients avoid these memories for a while until their brain is ready to process them again."

"In Shubman's case, we suspect that these stronger memories may involve Ishan. So, if you mention Ishan in front of him—or worse, if Ishan visits—those memories could be triggered right away." "What if he brings up Ishan?" Shahneel asked, feeling the situation was absurd.

"Then you simply steer the conversation away from that topic. It's not serious, really; it's just a precaution we need to take. I would recommend that you give him some space for the time being, but for now, just avoid mentioning Ishan, alright?"

"Okay," they replied. With that, they entered the room to greet Shubman. Although they had many questions, it seemed wiser to hold off for now. They just wanted to see Shubman awake and well, and thankfully, Ishan had gone along with Hardik earlier. They would contact Hardik to explain the situation and ask him to keep Ishan out of the hospital for at least three hours.

As they walked in, Shubman's mother rushed forward to hug him, while they stood back, witnessing the touching moment. "Are you feeling good? Does your head hurt?" she asked, concern etched on her face.

"I'm fine," he replied, smiling. He then looked at the others standing at the door and gestured for them to come in. Once they entered, the hugging and expressions of concern continued for a while. "I'm fine, guys; I'm not dead yet," Shubman said, finally addressing their worried expressions.

"You scared us all," Shahneel remarked after a moment. "Yeah, sorry about that," Shubman responded. They then shifted to various topics to keep the atmosphere light for a while.

Title: Realization
Chapter_Text:
Ishan was sitting on the balcony of Hardik's sea-facing apartment, sipping on the lemon juice given to him after lunch. He had been resilient to eat at first — stubborn and fidgety — but the sweet aroma drifting from the kitchen and the pleading faces around him had made him change his mind.

His mind had been racing for days now, conjuring up endless possibilities, weaving webs of confusion he couldn't untangle. Now, it needed to stop. Now, he needed a clear answer. Ishan had always believed he knew himself the best. Better than anyone else. But maybe he was wrong. Perhaps he never really knew what his heart desired. Or maybe... maybe he had known it all along and had chosen to feign ignorance.

Whatever it was, it was now playing with his mind. And he didn't appreciate it at all. He had always loved Shubman and his antics. He loved how they clicked instantly, how their conversations flowed without force, how the silences between them were never heavy. He loved how Shubman understood him even without words.

He loved the way Shubman's eyes sparkled when Ishan praised his batting stance or his passion for the things he loved. But...Was he in love with Shubman? There's a difference, Ishan thought bitterly. Loving someone and being in love with someone were two entirely different things. You could love someone deeply, cry for them, care for them endlessly — and still not be in love.

So, was he in love with Shubman? This wasn't the first time he had been thinking about it.

He had been randomly tortured by these thoughts ever since that reckless confession Shubman had pulled back in England. But no matter how much he thought about it, no matter how much he analyzed and dissected it — he just couldn't seem to find his answers.

"Kya soch raha hai?" Hardik's voice pulled him out of his spiral. The older man came up behind him, voice easy but eyes sharp. Ishan startled a little, then forced a smile. "Kuch nahi bhai." "Kuch nahi?" Hardik repeated, a sly smile tugging at his lips. "Bol de na kya hua hai. Apne bhai se bhi chupayega kya?"

Ishan just gave him a look. A look that said you know what's wrong, why are you making me say it? "Pata hai," Hardik admitted easily, "par tere muh se sunna chahta hoon main." Ishan exhaled heavily, defeated. "Shubman ke baare mein soch raha hoon aur kya." Hardik's grin widened. "Aur kya soch raha hai uske baare mein? Woh bol."

"Yaar bhai..." Ishan buried his face in his hands for a second, laughing miserably. Hardik just chuckled at his sorry state. "Bol jaldi." Ishan was trapped. He couldn't lie. He couldn't run. He hesitated, then mumbled, "Main soch raha tha..." He trailed off again, struggling.  Hardik raised an eyebrow, waiting patiently.

Ishan swallowed, cheeks heating. "Do I like him? I mean, obviously, I like him — but do I like him like... he likes me? You know... am I..." He couldn't finish. He just waved his hand helplessly in the air. Hardik chuckled again — soft, not mocking.

He leaned forward. "Alright," he said, voice steady. "Let's break it down." "How would you feel if you didn't speak to him for three days straight?" Ishan frowned. "That's... we speak almost every day. Of course, I'd feel bad if we didn't." Hardik nodded, unfazed.

"Okay. How would you feel if he didn't listen to something you said?" Ishan immediately shook his head. "Bhaiya, he always listens." "Exactly." A smirk tugged at Hardik's mouth. "And how about when he looks and pays attention to someone else when you're in the same room?" Ishan looked thrown "What?"

"Okay, okay. What about when he goes somewhere without informing you first?" "Bhai..." Ishan groaned, covering his face. Hardik grinned wider. "Or when he's upset and goes to talk to someone else instead of you. How would that feel?" There was no answer. Just a slight tightening around Ishan's mouth, a frown he couldn't suppress.

Hardik watched him carefully. Then he dropped the real bomb. "What if he went on a date with someone else after waking up? How would you feel then?" Ishan froze. Hardik leaned in, voice low, searching. "Ishan... what if he wakes up one day and denies ever having feelings for you? What if he says it was just some momentary lapse because of his memory loss? How would that make you feel?"

Ishan stared into the distance. He didn't say anything. The silence stretched long and thick between them. Hardik didn't rush him. He knew silence sometimes spoke louder than words. Finally, Ishan looked up — and there was something raw in his eyes. A vulnerability he rarely showed.

Hardik sat forward. "Ishan," he said gently. "Listen to me carefully now. You're sitting here worrying about how bad you'd feel if he didn't talk to you. You're defending how he always listens to you. How he always informs you. How he always looks for you." He smiled sadly.

"Tell me something — why do you think you care this much?" Ishan opened his mouth, then shut it. No answer. Hardik didn't need one. "Bhai, this isn't friendship anymore," Hardik said softly. "This isn't just 'oh, he's my bro.' This is you feeling something you're too scared to admit to yourself."

Ishan still looked confused, still defensive. Hardik leaned back, watching him calmly. "And don't even pretend you don't know it," Hardik said. "The way your face lights up when he texts. The way you search for him in a crowded room. The way you froze when I said he might go on a date."

Ishan swallowed thickly. Hardik's voice dropped even lower. "He already confessed bhai. He already told you what you mean to him. You're the one standing here pretending it's still just friendship." Ishan finally met his gaze — wide-eyed, lost.

"Friends don't look at friends the way you look at him," Hardik whispered. "Friends don't feel sick imagining someone else touching them. And friends damn sure don't hurt this much over the thought of losing them." Silence. The air felt electric.

"Accept it," Hardik said. "You're already gone for him. You just have to catch up with your own heart." Ishan's throat worked, struggling to swallow. Hardik leaned forward again. "Answer me this: Why does it hurt you so much when you imagine him with someone else?" Ishan shifted, uncomfortable.

"I don't know... it would just feel weird." "Really?" Hardik smirked. "Would you feel 'weird' if it were me?" Ishan blinked fast. "No... but you're different—" "Exactly." Hardik's grin turned triumphant. "Because it's not friendship with him. It's more." Ishan opened his mouth to argue — but no words came. He hated how much sense Hardik made.

"You're really gonna sit here and pretend it's normal, huh?" Hardik said, exasperated. "Normal friends don't get jealous. Normal friends don't stare at each other like they hung the damn stars." Ishan looked away stubbornly. "I don't know what I feel..." His voice cracked a little. "It's confusing. He forgot me, bhaiya. And even then... he found his way back to me."

Hardik's expression softened. "Baccha," he murmured. "Don't you get it? Even when his memories were wiped — even when fate tried to reset everything — his heart still chose you." He tapped Ishan's chest lightly. "Because it was always you." Ishan blinked rapidly, tears threatening.

"But what if he regrets it later?" he whispered. Hardik smiled sadly. "If forgetting you didn't change his heart... remembering sure as hell won't either." Ishan bit his lip hard, looking completely, utterly lost. Hardik sighed dramatically.

"Yaar, how dumb are you?" he said with mock scorn. "The man fell for you twice — from scratch. Meanwhile, you're sitting here acting like you caught feelings because of bad biryani." Ishan laughed — a broken, watery sound — cheeks turning pink. "You're scared because it's real," Hardik said quietly. "Because it's big. Because once you admit it — there's no pretending anymore."

Ishan stared at him, eyes wide and raw. Hardik's voice softened even more. "Stop thinking.

Stop being scared. You already know what you feel. You've always known. You were just waiting for him to say it first." "And now that he has," Hardik said, standing up and ruffling Ishan's hair affectionately, "you're out here acting like a goat who wandered into a science exam."

Ishan laughed helplessly, tears slipping out despite himself. Hardik smiled. "Go to him, idiot," he said fondly. "Go show him you love him too. You already have — in every way that matters." Ishan stood up too, heart pounding, adrenaline rushing through his veins. Hope blooming wild and fierce inside his chest.

Maybe he didn't have the perfect words. Maybe he didn't have all the answers yet. But he had one truth, blazing clear and undeniable. It was always him. It had always been Shubman. And now, finally, he was ready to chase what was his.

In the hospital, it had already been two hours since Shubman had woken up. He looked like he wanted to ask about something but he tried his hardest to not show it on his face. Everyone standing them knew what it was about they were all too tied to mention Ishan in front of him.

They just waited for it to be enough time for the doctors to check up on him again so that they could mention Ishan again and quench the mischieve that was bubbling in too much inside them. They were all just trying to make themselves useful to not ponder too much on the topic when Shubman spoke up finally having enough of the silence.

"where is he?" he asked looking towards shahneel with hopeful eyes. And it broke her heart a little having to divert the topic to something else. But just as she was about to put herself through that miserable conversation the doctors came inside again and asked everyone to evacuate the room for them to check on Shubman for the last time today,

Shahneel looked the happiest to leave the room. They all just looked at each other while outside. It was now just Virat, Rishabh, Abhishek, and Shubman's family now in the hallway in front of Shubman's hospital room. Rishabh was about to say something when he caught a glimpse of Hardik and Ishan walking in towards them.

They had informed Hardik about the Shubman situation explained by the doctors but it seemed that this was the most Hardik could keep Ishan busy. The doctors were still inside what if they came outside and asked them to not let Shubman meet Ishan yet? That would be tough but at least Ishan hadn't come before doctors could check up on Shubman. It was still good enough.

Also, like I said, I have been a bit too obsessed with Ishan's eyes lately. So, I got this really relatable poem from AI. I did NOT write this one but I would like to share it with you"ll. Here you go,

If the gods ever sculpted a masterpiece and forgot to name it,

they must have left it behind in the form of Ishan Kishan's eyes.

Those eyes —

bright enough to burn cities, soft enough to rebuild them.

A reckless blaze stitched into a gaze, dangerous in how easily they unravel every shred of logic you thought you had.

When he laughs, they crackle like a bonfire you want to throw yourself into;

when he focuses, they sharpen into weapons no armor could ever defend against.

You could write a thousand sonnets about the way they catch light,

you could fight a thousand wars just for one more second under their spell.

*They are not just eyes — they are commandments, they are chaos, they are church.*

And worst of all?

He doesn't even have the slightest idea that when he blinks, entire worlds fall to their knees.

Title: Mine?
Chapter_Text:
The silence in the corridor was palpable. They could all sense it; there was a shift in the air. Ishan looked like he was high on something, and they all looked at Hardik with curious eyes. Ishan was ever a charmer; he was anything but nervous. He was the sanctuary most people looked up to when under pressure.

The always bubbly, ever calm, the edge of serenity Ishan Kishan looked nervous. Or was he scared? Was it a concern on his face? They couldn't figure it out no matter how hard they tried, and they certainly couldn't ask him because that was definitely out of the question at the moment.

There were thousands of emotions swarming in his eyes, yet they couldn't pinpoint even one of them. They had expected Ishan to burst in here and yell at them for not informing him when Shubman woke up; they expected Ishan to act out of character, but this was something else.

The forced serenity and the uncomfortable calm were fucking with their brains; they needed Ishan to react, to shout and scream, or even to throw a little smile here or crack a joke there, but Ishan stood there near Shubman's door like his life was on the line. The emotions in his eyes turned off momentarily when the doctors left the room.

The female doctor who had taken it upon herself to make them as comfortable as possible walked towards them and smiled at Ishan. "They must have already informed you what was wrong, didn't they?" And before Ishan could conjure up words to answer her, she continued. It was almost as if she could sense the tension too.

"It is now all okay. Shubman had completely regained his memories without any complications, and you can meet him and talk to him like you would any other day without panicking." Then she smiled at Shubman's mother and left them to their elements.

They contemplated for a moment before collectively ignoring the eyes with which Ishan looked at Hardik. What they couldn't ignore was the shrug of Hardik's shoulder before he entered the room. Without any hesitation, without so much as a second thought. And behind him entered the entire crowd of people, with Ishan being the last of them all.

"Aur bhai, sab thik?" Hardik slapped Shubman's leg the moment he entered the room, and that earned him a slap on the arm by Virat, and the mood got lifted again, the awkward silence effectively ignored. "So, you remember everything now?" Rishabh asked stupidly. This earned him a lot of questioning glares.

"Yes, Rishabh, I do. You still doubt that?" Shubman asked, smiling at him, but then that smile suddenly got replaced by a frown the moment Shahneel walked a little further, pushing Hardik to one side and revealing the existence of Ishan. Shubman met Ishan's eyes, and the tension in the room felt suffocating.

Ishan looked like he wanted to say something, but all he did was smile at Shubman a bit too sweetly, while Shubman looked like he could combust on the spot at any moment. Shubman's cheeks were a brilliant shade of red, and he looked at Virat with eyes that had signs of SOS printed in them.

"I think I forgot to switch the AC off back at home, god the bill." Shubman's mother said and tried to leave in an extreme hurry while dragging her half-clueless husband with her. Shahneel looked like she had been holding her laugh for quite some time now, and like the good person she was, she decided to not laugh in her brother's face and embarrass him even more, so she too quietly exited while muttering a work excuse.

Virat took one look at Hardik's stupid smile and decided that that was it. He grabbed Hardik by the collar of his shirt and muttered something that suspiciously sounded like a curse before dragging him out of the room with himself. Rishabh stood there rooted on the spot, looking around like people had suddenly grown horns.

That was before Abhishek kicked him out the door before following him and closing the door behind them. Ishan and Shubman were very much aware of what was happening around them, and the last stunt that Abhishek had pulled, not even bothering to mask it for any sort of reason, had brought out a hollering laughter from Ishan, while Shubman just sat there looking mortified and at a complete loss for words.

As the door clicked shut behind Abhishek, a hush settled once again, softer this time, like the calm that follows a storm everyone had secretly been waiting for. Ishan's laughter trickled into a breath, and his gaze found Shubman's again.

Shubman, poor thing, looked like he'd been caught cheating in an exam — flushed, fidgeting, and very obviously regretting every bold decision his amnesia had encouraged him to make. "You," Ishan said, finally walking closer, arms folded, "look like you'd rather be facing a bouncer from Mitchell Starc than be here with me right now."

Shubman groaned, dragging a hand across his face. "That's because I would rather be doing that." Ishan let out a soft laugh again, but it didn't quite reach his eyes. He stopped by the edge of the hospital bed and looked at Shubman for a beat too long until the younger man fidgeted again.

"Tell me something," Ishan began, his voice gentle now, "do you remember what you said to me? Before... before everything came back?" Shubman looked like someone had turned gravity up on him. He straightened slightly, his eyes darting to Ishan's and then to the blanket he was now pretending to be intensely interested in. "I... I said a lot of things, I think."

"Right," Ishan nodded, stepping even closer now, voice steady. "But I'm asking about that thing. The one that made me forget how to breathe for a good ten seconds." Shubman sucked in a breath. "Ishan, I didn't— I mean, I wasn't thinking clearly. My head was all messed up, and maybe I said too much too fast—"

"Shubman," Ishan cut him off, and his tone made the younger man shut up instantly. "Don't do that. Don't backtrack now." There was silence. Ishan's voice was softer when he spoke again. "You forgot me, and you still found your way back. You didn't even remember who I was, and yet somehow... you still looked at me like I hung the damn stars in the sky."

Shubman's lips parted, stunned. "You don't get to do that, confess like that, and then take it back just because your memories rebooted. That's not how this works." Shubman blinked rapidly. "So what are you saying?"

Ishan looked away for a moment, then back at him, eyes glossy. "I'm saying..." He huffed, clearly frustrated at his own lack of words. "I'm saying I think I completely understand your feelings because maybe I felt it too, deep down. And maybe I was too dumb, or too scared, or too stubborn to see it for what it was. But when you forgot me and fell for me again—"

His voice broke a little, and he paused to swallow thickly. "—I realized you've always seen me in ways I've been too afraid to believe I deserve. And now all I can think about is how it took you forgetting me to remember us." Shubman looked like his entire operating system had shut down. He opened his mouth. Then closed it. Then opened it again. "Ishan..."

"No. Let me finish," Ishan said, stepping closer until their knees nearly touched. "When you said those things to me before your memories came back, you didn't sound confused, Shubman. You sounded free. Like, for once, your heart wasn't fighting your brain." Shubman's eyes were welling up now.

"Idiot," Ishan whispered, smiling through the tears threatening to spill. "You're my idiot. The annoying, batting-stance-obsessed, second-chance-taking idiot who apparently has to forget me just to admit he likes me." Shubman huffed a laugh, and a single tear ran down his cheek. "You're unbelievable."

"And you're slow," Ishan teased, gently wiping the tear with his thumb. "But you're also mine. If you still want to be." Shubman didn't respond with words. He leaned forward, shaky but sure, pressing their foreheads together, and whispered, "I wanted to be yours even when I didn't remember your name."

Ishan finally let out a teary chuckle. "You're such a sap." "Takes one to love one," Shubman murmured, and Ishan laughed again, the kind of laugh that cracked him open and healed him all at once.

Outside the door, a few very nosy teammates fist-bumped each other silently. And inside, in the sterile quiet of a hospital room, two people began something that had always been written for them — memory or not.

Title: Homecoming
Chapter_Text:
The confession was sweet, and it had left Ishan with a quiet kind of joy—the kind that settles deep in your chest and makes everything feel a little softer, a little lighter. He had been thinking about Shubman, about the confession that he had made since the England tour.

No matter how much he tried to ignore it, the overthinking came back to him every day. The thoughts of losing himself in search of something more had been haunting his dreams for some time now, but he also knew that the pushing back of the thoughts, the deliberate changing of focus, wasn't just because he didn't want to accept his feelings. Ishan was scared. Scared to hurt shubman--- again.

Ishan had known that something was charging between him and Shubman for some time now. He was trying to turn a blind eye to it, was trying to be as oblivious as possible, but there was no denying the fact that he had noticed the subtle touches and longing looks that Shubman threw his way.

Shubman...his shubman. Ishan knew him the best. Ishan would know if he was feeling uncomfortable even in the slightest without Shubman having to explicitly say it in words. Between him and Shubman, words had been overrated anyway. Shubman could take 0.02 seconds more to answer a question, and Ishan would know that there was something wrong.

Of course, he knew when Shubman started to feel something more than friendship towards him. At first, it had been a fleeting doubt, one that would often arise during an interview or maybe a net practice. A doubt that gave way to no further thinking or any kind of discomfort.

Then Ishan started to notice, along with the world, the prolonged stares and the touches that lasted longer than expected. The way Shubman's eyes found him in the crowd didn't feel the same as before; there was something different about the way Shubman reacted to his touches now.

Ishan had known; he had always known. It was his shubman after all. There was something desperate with the way Ishan went out of his way to avoid Shubman in the last few weeks before Shubman's accident. Don't get him wrong; he wasn't disgusted by Shubman or his feelings. No, Ishan was not disgusted or repelled. He was scared.

Ishan was scared of his own heart sometimes. He was scared that he would unknowingly hurt Shubman. He had thoughts, thoughts that would take up his entire nights and the majority of his consciousness and still wouldn't die down. Thoughts of harming their friendship in any way felt like torture beyond words for Ishan.

He had always thanked the heavens for Shubman. In the world of this competitive sport, he had not only gotten himself a close friend, a partner in crime, a loyal mate, or a personal cheerleader; no, Ishan had gotten himself home.

The late-night gossip, the ice cream parlor trips, the nightly movie marathons, and the religious phone calls had all hit home for Ishan, and he wouldn't trade them for anything. Not even the idea of having Shubman as something more could convince Ishan to risk a potential chance of ruining what they already had.

So no, Ishan wasn't disgusted; he was scared. Scared of ruining their friendship, scared of risking Shubman. Ishan was not a gambler, and if there was one thing that he would never gamble, it was Shubman. Ishan would choose death over having to lose Shubman. He had been so resolute in this resolve that he had overlooked the possibility of hurting Shubman unintentionally.

He had played dumb to all the love stares from Shubman, 'He had always looked at me like that; there is nothing different.' He told himself every time he caught Shubman staring. He had turned a deaf ear to all the teasings that Shubman got from their teammates. He had made fun of all the edited videos of him and Shubman online. Had laughed at the audacity of people to ship him with Shubman.

He blamed it on the weather when he felt hot after walking in on Shubman, who had just gotten out of a bath. 'It isn't like I haven't seen him half-naked before. Fuck the global warming.' He muttered under his breath after clumsily slamming the door shut. 'I have a heart issue; I am sure of it,' he thought after his heart beat a little too fast after that one eye contact with Shubman on the field.

During the last few weeks before the accident, Ishan was so immersed in avoiding his feelings and Shubman's feelings altogether that he hadn't noticed Shubman hurting just a bit more every time Ishan had refused an ice cream date, or a movie invitation, or something as simple as sitting with him at the dinner.

Ishan was so scared to hurt Shubman that he was hurting him trying to avoid the same. It had all led to that one fateful fight they had just before Shubman got into that accident. "Shubman, can you pass this list to Rohit Bhai?" Ishan said without looking up from his laptop. That is what he mostly did: kept himself busy and away from Shubman and his thoughts.

"Can you at least look at me when you ask me for something, or do you hate me so much that you can't even bear to look at my face?" Shubman said, his voice cracking just a bit at the end. That slip had made Ishan look at him with confusion etched on his face. "What?" "Do you hate me now?"

Shubman's resolve was at its end. He had waited for Ishan to come out of whatever he had been facing. To come to Shubman like he always did, but this has been too harsh for Shubman's heart. The more he tried waiting, the more it hurt him.  In the end, Shubman decided to confront Ishan.

"Why would I hate you, Shubman?" The use of his full name didn't help Shubman's breaking heart. He took the paper from Ishan's grip and went away to do what he was asked. "Shubman. Shubman, look at me." Ishan called behind him, but Shubman was adamant about not showing him how weak he was for him.

The air was thick with something more than just summer heat — it was the weight of too many things left unsaid. Shubman stormed down the steps of the stadium, jaw clenched, vision swimming, heart in his throat.

"Shubman, wait—!" Ishan called out, voice cracking as he stumbled after him. But Shubman didn't stop. He couldn't. Not after the way Ishan had been treating him lately — like a stranger. Like he didn't matter. Like none of what they'd shared ever happened.

His nails dug into his palms as he walked faster, trying to outrun the lump rising in his throat. The blur in his eyes had nothing to do with the humidity. "I'm talking to you, dammit!" Ishan shouted behind him, feet pounding against the pavement. Shubman didn't turn around.

"You don't get to act like I'm the problem when you're the one who's been shutting me out!" Ishan's breath hitched. "I was trying to protect you—!" "By hurting me?!" That was the first time Shubman's voice cracked, and it made Ishan freeze for a second. It was trembling and loud and thick with emotion.

Shubman didn't stop walking. "You think you're protecting me? By pretending I don't matter anymore? By looking through me like I'm nothing? God, Ishan. You're not protecting me. You're just... you're just breaking me." "Shubman, please," Ishan tried, picking up pace again. "I didn't mean to— I thought if I stayed away, it'd be easier—"

"For who?" Shubman shouted, whirling around now as he crossed the road, walking backward to face him. "Because it wasn't easier for me, Ishan! Not even for a second!" Ishan finally stepped into the road, distracted, focused only on him.

And in that awful second, Shubman's smile dropped. His pupils dilated. His voice came out in a panic-stricken scream. "ISHAN—!" Ishan barely had time to process it — the blur of headlights, the screech of brakes, the roar of a horn. Then shove. Cold wind. Screaming metal. Skin meeting pavement.

And silence. Time froze for a second.

Ishan blinked, dazed, looking around, trying to understand why the world tilted on its axis. Then his gaze dropped—and his heart left his body. "Shubman?" he whispered. Shubman was lying just a few feet away, motionless.

"SHUBMAN—"

Ishan shouted himself awake, yanked out of the memory that had been tormenting him for months. His hand was being held tightly—someone was there—but everything else was chaos. Voices swarmed around him, but he couldn't make out a single face. There was only darkness in front of him, thick and absolute. His eyes refused to open. Maybe he didn't want them to. Maybe he couldn't bear the thought of seeing Shubman lying there—still, fragile—all because he'd chosen to be an idiot.

There was bile rising in his throat. He wanted to cry, but even his tears had betrayed him. Breathing felt impossible. It was like the oxygen had been punched out of his system like grief had made a home in his lungs. He tried reaching out to the fleeting figures in the dark, desperate to find something to anchor him.

"Ishan, inhale." He followed the voice like it was gospel. He inhaled. "Exhale." He exhaled. "Inhale." He obeyed. "Exhale." With every breath, the oxygen slowly began to kick in—making him dizzy but conscious. "Look at me. Look at the torch."

There was a faint light ahead, a soft beam slicing through the blackness. Ishan tried to focus on it. His eyes strained, struggling to adjust until a face finally began to come into view.

The light grew brighter than expected, and he instinctively squinted. A bottle was pressed into his hands. He took a sip, his throat dry and scratchy, and looked up again. Concern was etched into every line of the person's face. Then came a light slap on his cheek, more grounding than alarming.

Just as he opened his mouth to react, a sharp pain flared up in his arm. "Ah—!" "You okay?" "What the fuck was that?" he rasped, breathless from the jolt. Before anyone could answer—before he could get a sense of who was around him—he was pulled into a hug.

Not just any hug. A hug that felt like home. He melted into it instantly, eyes stinging, throat closing. He clung to Shubman like he'd fall apart if he let go. And maybe he would've.

His innocent, sweet Shubman. His Shubman, who had fallen in love with him again even after forgetting who he was. His Shubman, who never once blamed him, not even after the memories returned. His Shubman, who, instead of yelling or asking for answers, was holding him like he was the one who almost lost everything. His Shubman, who had every right to be furious, to shout, to shove—but chose to stay soft.

His Shubman.

The tears weren't part of the plan. But they came anyway, hot and relentless. To be honest, the Shubman part of Ishan's life had never really followed any plan. He had the vague awareness that people were around—some concerned, others teasing—but none of them mattered. Not right now.

Nothing mattered when he was being held so close. When Shubman's heartbeat thumped steady and warm against his ear. When his presence alone was enough to calm the quake inside him. When the moment—the one that had shattered Ishan to the core—had finally passed. When Shubman was alive, safe, and right here.

That truth alone lulled Ishan toward sleep. A kind of sleep he hadn't known in months. The kind without worry. Without pain. In the arms of the person who meant more to him than breath itself.

This hadn't been on Ishan's to-do list. Not because he didn't want it—he just never thought he was lucky enough to deserve it. But sometimes life gives you more than you think you're worth. And maybe that's the quiet, aching beauty of wanting.

Of yearning.

Of finally having.

Title: Anchor
Chapter_Text:
When Ishan woke from the blissful slumber, the first thing he noticed was someone's arm protectively draped over him. He was being held so close, it almost hurt—whether from the grip of the person holding him or the aching swell in his chest, he wasn't sure. Maybe it was the overwhelming urge to cry when he realized it was Shubman holding him.

Anchored. In place.

He wanted to look up—to see Shubman's peaceful face, to make sure he wasn't hallucinating. But he also wanted to stay right where he was, in Shubman's arms, forever. He lingered in that dilemma for a while until a voice broke the silence—a sweet, velvety voice that could convince him to commit the most horrendous crimes, and he'd do them all in a trance.

"For how long are you going to pretend to be asleep?" Shubman asked with a soft chuckle. A chuckle that might've given Ishan a heart attack had his heart not already been somewhere else entirely. Ishan looked up, to see that stupidly perfect smile tugging at Shubman's lips. Abruptly, he sat up. "You're dangerous for my heart, Shubi."

Shubman thought that was utterly ridiculous—everyone knew exactly who was dangerous for whom—but he smiled anyway. "You scared me. What happened earlier?" he asked, resting his head on Ishan's shoulder. At that question, the memories hit Ishan like a hurricane.

"Shubman," his voice cracked. Ishan turned to face him, taking his hand in both of his own. "Shubi..." It was hard to speak. Shubman was looking at him like an obedient puppy, eyes soft and open, the eyes he loved so much. "I'm so sorry," Ishan whispered. It was all too much.

"Shhh, baby... You don't have anything to be sorry for." Shubman pulled him into another hug, and Ishan broke. Again. He was sobbing like he'd lost everything—and maybe he had. Maybe everything that was now finally within his grasp had once been so far out of reach, that it had broken him.

"No, Shubi. It was my fault. I shouldn't have pushed you away. I shouldn't have avoided the confrontation. I thought I was keeping you safe. I thought I was protecting our friendship. But—fuck—I don't know why I ever believed I could keep anything safe at all."   "Shh. Stop speaking nonsense, Ishu. You are my safe place. I feel safest when I'm with you. If you can't keep something safe, then no one can. Okay?"

"Look at you—still trying to cheer me up when I almost got you killed." Ishan's body shivered at the word killed, and it shattered something inside Shubman. "No, Ishan. Look at me." His voice held a quiet command that Ishan couldn't disobey. He lifted his gaze.

"You are the love of my life. Do you understand that, Ishan Kishan? If I had the chance to go back and redo everything, I'd still choose to save you. Do you know why? Because I love you. I have always loved you. Even before I knew what love meant, you were the one I loved the most."

"You avoided me because you were trying to protect us—what we had. You were doing the right thing in your own way. But let me tell you something: I could die for you, Ishu. But don't ever ask me to stay away from you. That would kill me more than death ever could." Ishan placed a hand over Shubman's mouth then, trying to stop him from speaking any more about death.

"Let me finish." Shubman gently moved Ishan's hand away. "I was mad at you. Yes. But not because you owe me anything—not because you're obligated to talk to me. I was mad because you decided what was best for me without asking me. Because you, Ishan Kishan, decided that being away from me was the best thing for me."

"That is the most brilliantly stupid thing in the world because even a stranger would know—you being away from me for even a second takes a year off my life."

"You, pretty little human, are the bane of my existence. I'd rather learn to live without oxygen than try to live without you. And none of this—none of what happened—was your fault. I jumped in for you, and I would unapologetically do it a thousand times over. You breathing, you being happy—that's all I need. With me or without me."

If Ishan was crying like a toddler throwing a tantrum, well—that was between him, Shubman, and the quiet safety of this hospital room. How had he been so lucky? How had he ever managed to deserve Shubman?

"Repeat after me: I—" Ishan looked at him like he'd grown horns but still repeated. Because when had he ever denied Shubman anything? "I." "Am. Not. Responsible." "I am not responsible." Ishan echoed, voice already cracking. "For your accident." "For your accident." His voice nearly broke on the last word.

"You hear that? You are not responsible for anything that happened to me. You're not responsible for me being in love with you. You're not responsible for me being stupid. And you are definitely not responsible for my accident. Do you understand?" Ishan could only nod. Because words—what even were they?

Meanwhile,

"Ishan looked awful, waking up earlier." Siraj just put it out there in the universe because why was no one talking about the state at which Ishan had woken up screaming Shubman's name earlier? That shook him to his core, and looking at the others, they looked like this wasn't the most heart-wrenching thing they had witnessed today.

"Thank God that trauma is kicking him. He needed that awful waking up, to be honest." Shahneel spoke, making everyone nod. "Don't even remind me how Ishan had looked just after the accident. He looked lifeless like his life was on the line." Hardik said, clutching his head. That memory of Ishan is haunting him.

"To be honest, his life WAS on the line," Shahneel said, stating the obvious.   Shubman's mother looked up from the tea she hadn't touched. "He really thought he was responsible?" Abhishek nodded slowly. "Didn't say it out loud at first. But it was all over his face. You could see it in the way he flinched every time someone brought up the accident."

"He looked like he was punishing himself just by breathing," added Pallavi. "Like if he dared to smile, it would be some sort of betrayal." There was a pause. The kind that stretches because everyone in it is trying not to cry again. "He wouldn't even come near me," said Shahneel, voice soft. "Every time I tried to talk to him, it was like he thought I was going to blame him. As if we could ever..."

"God, it hurt," said Shubman's father, shaking his head. "It hurt watching that boy fall apart over and over again, thinking we'd hate him. That we'd blame him. As if we hadn't seen the way he'd been breaking from the moment it happened."

"He never blamed Ishan," Shubman's mother said. "Not even once. Not when we were waiting outside the OR. Not when the doctors were unsure. He kept whispering his name." "And Ishan probably thought he didn't deserve it." She wiped her eyes. "All this time he carried that weight alone. That kind of guilt eats you alive."

"He needed someone to say it. Out loud. That it wasn't his fault." "He needed Shubman to say it," Pallavi added. "No one else would have mattered." Another beat of silence passed.

"I saw him today," said Hardik quietly. "Right after, Shubman hugged him. His face... you could see the moment it cracked. The exact moment the guilt let go. Like he'd been underwater this whole time and someone finally pulled him up."

Shubman's dad gave a small smile. "He loves him." "They love each other," his mother corrected gently. And this time, the silence in the room felt a little lighter. They all were exceedingly aware of the turmoil that Ishan went through right after the accident. He had spent the majority of his days hanging around in the hospital.

He refused to meet or talk to anyone. They remembered how one of the doctors had to physically drag Ishan into the canteen and explicitly threaten him into eating something. He had been lifeless. They remembered how his mother had to cry actual tears before Ishan agreed to go on that tour with the team.

They remembered how no one batted an eye or blamed Ishan even for a second when he ran right off the field onto the plane when he got informed about Shubman waking up. "We tried to keep the information of Shubman waking up from him for as long as possible. Stupid me slipped it up. He just left everything important behind for Shubman, didn't he?" Shahneel said, chuckling.

"He left everything not so important behind for a very important Shubman." Ishan's mother added, looking at Shahneel with a small smile. They had all silently agreed upon their underlying love for each other. Never commenting on the obvious. They wanted their children to be happy and very much together.

"I remember the day we were checking Shubman out. It was the 18th of July," Shahneel said, her voice a little steadier now like the memory was wrapped in gold. "Someone mentioned the date, and Shubman—he turned so fast like his body knew before his brain caught up. He said, 'There's something special on the 18th, isn't there?' I had goosebumps. Not kidding. He'd forgotten almost everything but somehow remembered there was something about that day."

Shubman's mom let out a small, teary laugh. "That boy and his 18th of July. You'd think it was a national holiday." "It was to him," Ishan's mother added. "We all remember the way he used to light up when the date came close. He'd start planning two weeks in advance." "Because it was Ishan's birthday," Pallavi said, almost reverently.

"And he remembered it," Shahneel whispered. "Even through everything, even when he couldn't recall his own passwords—he remembered Ishan."

Everyone went quiet again. But it wasn't the uncomfortable kind of silence. It was full. Like hearts had just collectively sighed in relief. Like the ache had stretched itself and made space for something softer. They all sat with that for a moment.

"I walked in this morning," Ishan's mother added after a while, "and saw the two of them curled up in that tiny hospital bed. Shubman holding him like his life depended on it, and Ishan sleeping like he hadn't in weeks. Like someone had finally taken the weight off his chest."

"Someone had," said Shubman's mom, her voice warm. "Love did." Virat stretched his legs and groaned. "God, I feel like I've aged ten years watching these two self-destruct and rebuild like it's their favorite hobby." "I'm just glad they chose love over fear," Pallavi said. "And over guilt. That's harder than it sounds."

And they all sat there in comfortable silence. Basking in the knowledge that these two idiots had finally grown some brains. That they had finally accepted the obvious.

Title: Admirer
Chapter_Text:
Today was the big day. Today, Shubman was getting discharged from the hospital. His family, along with Ishan's, was packing up everything around him. Shubman's father was at the reception desk, clearing out any pending bills, and his mother was carefully organizing his medications, consulting a doctor about the effects and side effects of each one. She didn't want anything that could jeopardize Shubman's health in even the smallest way.

Ishan's mother was helping Shahneel pack some of Shubman's things, and Ishan's father was deep in conversation with Rohit, discussing all the ways Shubman's recovery might impact his career. Everyone was busy doing something—everyone but Shubman.

He sat idly on the hospital bed, his back resting against the headboard, a soft blanket pooled around his legs. His gaze, however, was anything but idle. He was staring intently at Ishan, who stood in the corner of the hospital room, engaged in a flurry of words over the phone. His fingers gestured animatedly, brows furrowed in deep frustration or concern.

Shubman had thought Ishan was beautiful countless times before. And objectively, it was true. His thoughts weren't just biased by the overwhelming feelings he had for the man. No. Ishan Kishan was breathtakingly beautiful, crush or not. That wasn't up for debate. That was the very first thing Shubman had realized after losing his memory.

He remembered seeing Ishan's face that day and thinking, almost absentmindedly, just how pretty he was. He had brushed it off as a simple, harmless observation. Nothing more. Nothing deeper. But it had turned out that Shubman had always been hopelessly in love with him.

Now, as he sat and watched Ishan pace with frustration painted on his face, Shubman knew that Ishan wasn't just beautiful—he was beautiful that had poems written about them. And if only Shubman were talented enough, he would have written a few himself.

He had certainly tried. He thought about it more times than he could count. But every time he sat down to write something, the words would betray him. They'd feel clumsy, insufficient, and painfully unworthy. He wasn't sure if it was his lack of poetic skill or if it was that the words themselves didn't dare to insult Ishan's beauty by trying to describe it.

Maybe that was it. Maybe Ishan didn't need words to be described. Perhaps the very concept of beauty had needed someone like Ishan to finally make sense. Perhaps the words weren't shy—they were simply speechless in front of him.

And now, with his memories restored, Shubman was grateful. Because he never wanted to forget anything that had Ishan in it. Not a single thing. He wanted to remember every little detail. He wanted to know every quirk, every favorite food, every dream, and every fear. He wanted to tattoo Ishan's preferences onto his mind.

He wanted to wake up every morning and look at Ishan's face as he slept beside him. He wanted to memorize the way his eyes looked when they were closed. Those lashes, those lids. He wanted to learn the exact curve of them—the slight almond shape of his eyes when they were tucked away behind tan, soft lids.

He wanted to memorize the gentle arc of Ishan's lashes as they cast shadows across his cheeks, to study the quiet stillness in his expression when he was asleep. Even then, even with his fire tucked away, Ishan still looked powerful. Like a resting god.

That image—of Ishan's closed eyes, peaceful and perfect—was one Shubman wanted to be braided into the strands of his memory. He wanted to be able to paint thousands of portraits, each one capturing the silent grace of those eyelids. The unspoken serenity of a man who was so alive, so vibrant, even when still.

Ishan had that kind of silent beauty. The kind you didn't gawk at—you just observed in awe. Shubman had always been one of those observers. But unlike the rest of the world, he wasn't just another passerby in Ishan's orbit. No, he took pride in being the first one—the first observer. He was a worshipper.

He wanted to outline the shape of Ishan's perfect nose with his fingers, to press his palm gently to those warm cheeks every morning. But he couldn't. As close as they were, Shubman didn't have that right. He wasn't allowed to touch Ishan like that. Not yet. So, instead, he stared.

Stared until he memorized every bump and ridge of Ishan's face. Stared until he could trace the location of every mole, every tiny freckle with his eyes closed. He had always been silently observing Ishan. Always appreciating him in his own quiet way. Shubman had always been the first to wake up between the two of them.

Not because he was a morning person—he was anything but that—but because he didn't want to waste a single chance to watch Ishan sleep. Silently appreciate that serene beauty. He wasn't an addict, but maybe he was addicted to Ishan. To his beauty, his warmth, his everything. Maybe he didn't fully understand the fine line between admiration and obsession when it came to him—but could you blame him? It was Ishan Kishan.

"Pretty, isn't he?" The voice was soft and delicate, but it startled Shubman out of his thoughts. He turned to see a woman standing a little distance away. Her expression was serene, almost fond, and her eyes were fixed on Ishan with an emotion that mirrored his own.

She had dimples on her cheeks and soft features that made her look kind. Shubman braced himself for the sharp pang of jealousy—but it didn't come. Instead, he found himself smiling. "The prettiest," he said quietly, sincerely. She glanced at him, and their eyes met. Dimples met dimples in a silent agreement.

"Hi. I'm Ira," she said, offering her hand. "I was the assistant doctor on your case." "Hello, Ira. I'm Shubman." He shook her hand gratefully. Her grip was warm, grounding. There was a kind of mutual understanding between them—a shared reverence for the boy standing in the corner of the room, still arguing passionately over the phone.

Ira had the kind presence that the family craved while their loved ones fought life-threatening situations inside the OT. She had a calm presence and an indelible personality. Her smile held a silent promise, and her hands were ready to hold onto any shivering ones. She looked like the person you could gossip with all you wanted, but she also looked like the person who would fight for you until the end. Her presence held a sense of calm in the chaos.

Shubman would usually be jealous of anyone who imitated that soft fondness for Ishan on anyone else. With Ira, on the other hand, he didn't feel threatened at all. He didn't feel the need to protect Ishan or the need to prove anything at all. When she looked at him, he felt seen, as if she knew his deepest secrets. And although he felt so exposed, he didn't feel threatened. In fact, he felt understood.

He felt as if he didn't need to explain himself for her to understand. He felt as if she knew. She couldn't possibly have known anything, but he felt it anyway. He didn't hate this feeling at all. Ira smiled at him in appreciation. He didn't know if he was being appreciated for recovering or if it was because of Ishan.

"I've seen how he sat by your bedside for hours when you were unconscious," she said gently. "Didn't let anyone move him. Not even once." Shubman's eyes immediately landed on Ishan, who was now looking right at him. Their eyes met, and Shubman passed him a small smile before turning back to her. "What?" he feigned confusion. He knew he couldn't lie to her, but he tried anyway.

"Oh, please, Shubman. Everyone knows," she chuckled, like an adult amused by a child trying to lie about breaking a vase. "Knows what?" He gave her his best-confused expression. She just smiled, dimples deepening. "Knows how head over heels you are for him."

Shubman choked on plain air. God, he hadn't expected her to be that blunt. "You aren't one to beat around the bush, I see," he said, swallowing his embarrassment. "Aww, there's nothing to be embarrassed about. I mean, I completely get you. It's Ishan, after all." She looked over at Ishan with the same fondness again, and Shubman turned to her, curious.

"Fan?" he asked. She shook her head. "Admirer." He let out a suppressed chuckle. "So, what do you admire? Cricket or him?" She looked offended. "Him, of course. Is there anything else even worthy of admiration?" And Shubman couldn't help but agree. "Since when have you been this 'admirer'?" he asked, brow furrowed.

"Since his Under-19 days. Wish I knew him even before that," she said, almost dazed. "I remember the day they brought you in after your accident. I was the one who received your barely breathing body. You were my first severe case, Shubman, and I was just as scared as the friends who had brought you."

"I did all I could to keep you breathing until the doctors arrived. I remember preparing the OT. I remember checking on your flickering breath. It had been a hectic week—all the doctors were at the staff quarters. And I don't know if it was your luck or mine, but a massive tree decided to fall right in front of the quarters that day."

"I nearly gave up. It was too much. I hadn't handled something like that before. I had no idea if I was doing it right. Then I saw him. You wouldn't believe it, but I hadn't even realized who you were until then."

"I didn't understand why everyone was panicking or why I was ordered to shift you to the VIP section. I just knew I had a patient to save. But then, I saw him. Sitting outside your room, looking like the life had been sucked out of him. Like he'd just lived through the worst day of his life."

"And then I saw you—really saw you. Shubman Gill. And suddenly, everything made sense. But that wasn't why you became my priority. You became my priority because of the way he looked at you as if his breath was tied to yours."

"You don't sound like just an admirer. Have you met him before?" Shubman asked. "I have," she smiled. "When?" he asked quickly. Ira chuckled.

"It was just for a moment. I was never into cricket. My family would watch matches while I'd escape into chores. I thought it was the most overrated game in existence," she said, smirking at his offended expression. "So how did you know him from his Under-19 days?"

"I had friends who were crazy about cricket. The year I got into my dream medical college was the same year my parents died in a car accident. Inadequate care. The doctors failed them. After that, my friends did everything to get me out of my shell. One day, they dragged me to a practice match."

"I didn't want to go. But they forced me. After the game ended, they went to meet the players. I stayed behind. And that's when he came and sat beside me. I didn't notice him at first. When I finally did, I wasn't hostile. Just... calm."

"That wasn't common back then. I was a scared, shattered version of myself. But he made me feel safe. He smiled and asked me if I was okay. I didn't reply. He kept talking anyway, a thousand questions I didn't answer. But he didn't stop."

"He was full of energy—sunshine in human form. I just stared, trying to make sense of his words. I physically recoiled from his positivity. He talked for what felt like hours, even if it was just minutes. I couldn't understand anything."

"But then, looking into his eyes at that moment, I felt safe—seen. I had been wanting to cry ever since that accident. Crying heals something that makes your chest feel heavy, they say. But I couldn't. No matter how much I tried, I just couldn't. But then, at that moment as I looked into his eyes, I started crying. Tears started flowing freely--unrestrained. I felt free. I didn't even know I had it in me."

"He pulled me into a hug. A stranger. Warm and grounding. I poured my heart out, and he listened. He convinced me to see a therapist. That was the turning point for me. He healed something he hadn't broken, and for that, I'll always be thankful."

"Cricket suddenly mattered. Every six he hit stitched my heart a little more. I didn't even know his full name back then. Just that he was Ishan, a cricketer. That was enough. And when I saw him that day, lifeless outside your room, it hit me hard. The light in him had dimmed."

"So I gave everything I had to save you, Shubman. Because you matter to him. And anything that matters to Ishan Kishan automatically matters to me."

Shubman had unshed tears in his eyes. He had always known that Ishan was sunshine personified, and he had also known that Ishan had saved him at bad times before. But there was a difference between trying to lighten up your best friend's life and trying to heal a complete stranger. Shubman was so in love with Ishan that it wasn't even possible to fall more for him, but in this moment he fell more anyway.

He looked at Ishan with the most delicate look, as if he would disappear in thin air if he stared a bit too hard.  Ira looked back at Ishan and smiled to herself. "You're lucky." "I am," Shubman agreed, his eyes finding Ishan again. "I really am." He didn't even try to question it at all. He was the luckiest, and he took pride in accepting it.

They both stood in silence for a moment, watching Ishan. He was no longer talking—just standing there, running a hand through his hair, visibly exhausted. He looked over and caught Shubman's gaze. For a second, their eyes locked, and everything else faded away.

Ishan walked over to him, the tiredness in his expression immediately melting into something softer as he approached. "Everything's done. We can leave in fifteen," he said. "Good," Shubman said, trying to keep his tone casual despite the flutter in his chest. Ishan gave him a curious look, then added, "You okay?" Shubman smiled up at him. "Never better."

Ishan didn't look convinced at all, but he decided to let it go for the moment. Then his gaze landed on Ira, who was standing behind Shubman with glossy eyes. She immediately passed him a smile as their eyes met. "You have dimples." Ishan pointed at the fact, and her dimples deepened.

"Oh. Sorry, it's just that you were the doctor who helped throughout this process, and I know you, but I hadn't realized that you had dimples until this moment, and I just point at it. I just—" Ishan started to frantically explain when he realized how awkward it sounded. Ira's laugh cut Ishan's uncalled rant short.

She extended her hands towards his head but then frowned a bit and pulled her hand back. "I am Ira, by the way." She extended the same towards Ishan for a hand, and he shook it with a smile. "That name sounds familiar. Have we met before?" And nobody had to know if her heart did a somersault at that. Shubman gave her a knowing look, but Ishan didn't dwell on that any longer.

"It's just you look familiar. Anyway, you have helped a lot. Thank you." Ishan shook her hand a bit more, and she smiled gratefully at him. "Happy to help." He took Shubman's file. She took a quick look and left the room, throwing Shubman a look from over her shoulder. Shubman didn't realize what that look meant until after he reached his home and opened his file. She had left him a note inside.

He loves you, Shubman. You're his priority—I hope you truly understand that. I told you how lifeless he looked when they first brought you here, how it felt like all the light had drained out of him. But there's more you need to know—more you need to see.

You should see the way he looks at you when you're out there playing your best innings. His eyes shine, not just with pride, but with this quiet kind of awe, like he's watching someone he's always believed in proving the world right. And even when you're being utterly ridiculous—doing something that makes no sense to anyone but you—he still looks at you with so much love it's almost unfair.

He softens around you. There's this ease in him that doesn't exist when you're not near. He smiles more freely. He breathes a little easier. When you walk off the field uninjured, there's this almost imperceptible sigh of relief in him—as if just your being okay brings him peace.

So don't ever doubt how much you mean to him. Because I've seen it—when you're around, he doesn't just exist, Shubman. He lives.

And listen carefully, because I'm only going to say this once—if you ever hurt him, even unintentionally, you'll have an army of people coming for you. And I promise I'll be at the front of that line, sharpening my teeth. This isn't a threat, Shubman—no, no—it's more of a... gentle warning wrapped in concern and laced with just enough menace to keep you on your toes. I've seen how you look at him when you think no one's watching. I've heard you mumble his name even when you were unconscious. You fell for him without even remembering him—that's how hopelessly gone you are. And honestly? Who could blame you? It's Ishan. People trip over themselves for him without a single word from his side.

But here's the thing—he's soft in ways most people don't see. He's quieter with his pain. So don't you dare be the reason he hurts. Not even a little. Not even by accident. You don't get to love him halfway. If you're in, you're all in. Got it?

And I know you, Shubman. I know you probably understand him better than most people ever will. I know you'd never willingly hurt him—not him, not the one who means the world to you. And I believe that. I trust you with that. But still... consider this a warning, just in case. Not because I doubt you, but because he's too important to leave unprotected. So even if it's unintentional, even if it's a moment of weakness—don't let yourself be the reason he breaks.

It was the most valid warning he had ever read. It made him more happy than scared. He looked over at Ishan sitting with his family and realized how happy it made him feel that this amazing human was loved like how he deserved to be loved. And he would willingly get beaten up if he ever dared to hurt this precious person.

His eyes met with Pallavi's, and she threw him a questioning look, which he immediately avoided by looking away. He doubted that everyone knew about his feelings for Ishan, but he wasn't ready to come clean in front of them yet. So, he did what was needed; he avoided all kinds of confrontation.

Just as he looked back where she was sitting, his eyes met with Ishan's, and he clutched his chest, thinking that he might as well be dead one of these days.

Enjoy this extra-long chapter as a small compensation for my recent absence. I hope you didn't have to wait too long. Thank you for your patience. I will be completing this fanfic soon, so stay tuned. If you liked the chapter, please take a moment to vote on it.

Love to you'll.

Title: Safe With You
Chapter_Text:
Okay, so my last chapter turned out to be a bit too long, filled with a lot of things that probably didn't need to be there. I just want to say I'm sorry for that—it honestly just wrote itself, completely bypassing my brain in the process.

This chapter, though, is the one I've been trying to write for the longest time. It took its own sweet time, and I think the previous (overwritten) chapter was necessary in its own way just to build up to this moment, even if it felt like a detour.

So, yeah—apologies if I disappointed anyone. Please bear with me. I'm not a writer, not really. In fact, I often feel like I'm pretty bad at it. But I'm trying.

"You look so deep in thought these days; what is wrong, Shubi?" Ishan asked one night as they sat on the balcony of Shubman's room in Chandigarh. Shubman and Ishaan, along with Shubman's family, had recently returned to their hometown. Ishan had stayed with Shubman while in Mumbai, and he went with them when they proposed to stay at home for some time before either of them had to go back to cricket.

"nothing." Shubman had replied, but he didn't look back at Ishan. He kept looking in the distance, thinking about something. It had become too common for Shubman these past few weeks to zone out. Although it was not uncommon or unusual, its appearance had become too frequent lately.

Shubman would look at you and talk to you, but he wouldn't be fully into the conversation. It was as if something was so awake in his mind that he kept drifting off from reality sometimes. Everyone had noticed it. They were all concerned about Shubman, of course, but they also knew that coming back from the state that he had been in was not an easy task and would definitely have some side effects on Shubman's conscious.

So, they had tried to ignore it as much as possible, but now they couldn't ignore it anymore. So, Ishan had decided to break the ice. "Shubi," he said softly, scooting closer, his arm wrapping around Shubman's shoulder. "If something is bothering you, please tell me." Shubman leaned into him but said nothing. Ishan didn't push. He just stayed—warm, steady, present.

Ten minutes passed in silence. Then, without warning, Shubman turned and pulled Ishan into a bone-crushing hug. "I love you," he whispered into Ishan's neck, his voice trembling.

Ishan just smiled into his shoulder and hugged back tighter. Then suddenly, as if he realized something, Ishan's smile dropped for a second, and he pulled back from the hug, cradling Shubman's face in his palm. "You've been overthinking this, haven't you? this whole time?" And Shubman remained silent. "Baby, look at me," Ishan said gently, tilting his face until their eyes met. "You have to talk to me. I can't help you if you shut me out."

And finally, after days of silence, Shubman met his gaze fully. Their eyes locked, and for a heartbeat, the world stilled. Shubman's eyes were glassy, swimming with unsaid words. He opened his mouth to say something, but nothing came out. Shubman had been thinking about his future with Ishan and about that fateful confession at the hospital.

Shubman had loved Ishan for the majority of the time that he had spent with him. Now that he finally had his Ishan with him, the thoughts of family and people finally decided to make an appearance. It was not like Shubman hadn't thought about all this before, but back then he saw no possible chance of them coming together. He didn't think, even in his wildest of dreams, that Ishan would ever reciprocate his feelings. And so, the thoughts of what people might think had always been secondary in Shubman's mind.

It was not the same anymore. Now, Shubman had Ishan to himself, and nothing else mattered for some time before he realized that he had two families to confess his sexuality to and tons of friends who didn't know about him yet. He had forgotten that he was a public figure in a country where people valued not just your game but also your moral character and everything that is not in relation to the sport you play at all.

He forgot that he was a cricketer in a country that worshipped cricket and wouldn't let anyone live that taints it even by a bit. Shubman had always thought that these things didn't affect him at all, that he wouldn't give two fucks about people if they had a problem with him and his sexuality. But it wasn't the same anymore.

Shubman wasn't at his prime anymore. He had just gotten back from an accident that could have very well taken his life. He was vulnerable and weak. And he wasn't alone. When he had thought about people knowing his sexuality and judging him it was just him that was going to be judged and bashed because he was the only one in the picture.

Now, though, there was also Ishan. He would die before he would let anything touch Ishan and his character. He had never imagined a possibility where Ishan would be his, and therefore he never thought of the scenario where he would need to be dragged into Shubman's drama. But now that Ishan was also a part of this, it was scarier than thought itself.

There were butterflies in his stomach every time he thought about Ishan reciprocating his feelings, but now there was just anxiety rolling in his veins. He wished to be as brave as his forgotten self. He wished that he didn't use his brain at all. But unfortunately for him, that was not how things worked. He had to be present, and he had to think things through. He had to be brave, but he also had to be strategic.

"Shubman, say it," Ishan demanded, and Shubman broke. "I—Ishi, I don't think that it is as easy as it looks. I can't endanger your career." And Ishan frowned at that before a majestic smile appeared on his angelic face. Shubman looked at Ishan, who sat there trying to stifle his laughter.

"Are you the same person from before? Because honestly, Shubman, you didn't seem very concerned about my career when you decided that it was a good time in the middle of the practice field around all the other players present there to ask me, 'Have I always been romantically into you, or have I started losing my mind behind you now?'"

That one sentence brought back the memories that Shubman desperately tried to forget. That made all the blood in his body rush to his head and make him a bit dizzy. Shubman groaned, burying his face in his hands. "Can we not talk about that?" he asked in a small voice, almost wishing that the void would swallow him right that moment.
